text: Welcome to this video of a robotic right upper lobe lobectomy with chest wall resection, performed by Vijay Joshi and Joel Dunning and edited by Jason
We present the case of a seventy five year old female with a highly PET positive peripherally located tumour.
As a result, the plan was to perform a wed resection of the tumour and send for frozen section, with scope to perform a full chest wall resection if there were inadequate margins.
The surgeon initiates staple firings to perform a wedge resection of the peripherally located tumor in the right upper lobe.
The stapler in use being that it's produced by Lexington Medical, which makes staplers that can fit the Covidian gun, but at a thirty percent less cost.
The surgeon performs the final staple firing to complete the wedge resection of the peripheral lung tumor.
The surgeon begins resecting the chest wall, aiming for clear margins, using a spatula in one arm and cadia forceps in the other to dissect around the second and third ribs.
Following the completion of the wedge resection, the surgical team prepares to proceed with the right upper lobe lobectomy, while awaiting the frozen section results.
The surgeon uses a Covidian Tan stapler to transect the right upper lobe vein during the pulmonary lobectomy procedure.
The surgeon advances to the truncal branch of the pulmonary artery, utilizing a Tan-45 Covidian stapler to secure the vessel.
The surgeon begins the dissection of the subcarinal station seven lymph node, carefully separating it from surrounding tissues.
The patient had a previous mediastinoscopy which showed this to be negative.
The surgeon begins the removal of the station R-10 lymph node, located anterior to the pulmonary artery and inferior to the azygos vein.
The surgeon places a sling around the right upper lobe bronchus, visually isolating it from surrounding structures.
A leader cath was initially trialed.
The surgeon uses a curved tip stapler for the final firing, securing the tissue in the surgical field.
The posterior ascending branch of the pulmonary artery is visible in the surgical field.
The surgeon completes the arterial and bronchial firings, indicating the finalization of vessel and airway division in the pulmonary lobectomy procedure.
The surgeon proceeds to dissect the fissure between the upper and middle lobes of the lung, preparing to separate these anatomical structures.
The surgeon completes the firings from a posterior to anterior direction, finalizing the separation of the lobes.
After the final firing with a curve tip stapler, the surgeon places the resected pulmonary lobe into a surgical bag for removal.

Following this, the results of the frozen section came back showing that there was a nought point one millimetre margin, which was deemed inadequate, therefore we proceeded with the chest wall resection.
The surgeon exposes the second and third ribs by approaching posteriorly, revealing the anatomical structures in the surgical field.
The surgeon uses a jiggly wire, passing it behind each rib to facilitate further dissection or retraction.
The surgeon identifies each intercostal artery and uses diathermy to cauterize them, ensuring hemostasis during the chest wall resection.
An additional incision is visible just underneath the axilla, complementing the previous surgical approach.
The surgeon uses a jiggly saw, stabilized by robotic arms, to cut through the exposed ribs, demonstrating precise control and gentle technique.
The surgeon uses a jiggly saw to cut through the third rib, following the exposure of the second and third ribs.
The surgeon uses a jiggly saw to cut through the second rib posteriorly, with robotic arms stabilizing the instrument during the procedure.
There are other ways that this can be done.
However, we did not have any endoscopic rib shears or burrs.
The surgeon uses a jiggly saw, stabilized by robotic arms, to cut through the rib, demonstrating the chosen technique for addressing the rib section.
The surgeon uses an axillary incision and a rib cutter to cut the ribs anteriorly after previously cutting them posteriorly.
The surgeon dissects the ribs from the muscles of the anterior chest wall through the axillary incision, with the assistant pushing the ribs to facilitate cutting along the correct anatomical plane.
The surgeon completes the dissection of the second and third ribs, which are positioned under the scapula, without the need for patching.
Final histology revealed that there was no residual tumour remaining in the chest wall, ensuring that we'd achieved adequate margins in this T-III N-naught M-naught tumour resection.
The external view shows the placement of standard robotic ports along with an additional auxiliary incision on the chest wall.
